text: This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English. I'm Neil. And I'm Bekka.
zh: 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。我是Neil。我是Bekka。

text: Neil, what do the following animals have in common? Zebras, giant pandas and magpies.
zh: Neil,下面这些动物有什么共同点?斑马、大熊猫和喜鹊。

text: That's pretty easy, Bekka. They're all black and white.
zh: 这很简单,Bekka。它们都是黑白相间的。

text: Right. But do you know why these animals evolved to be black and white?
zh: 没错。但你知道这些动物为什么会进化成黑白色吗?

text: I'm not so sure. Probably something about survival.
zh: 我不太确定。可能跟生存有关吧。

text: That's usually the answer, isn't It is. The animal world is full of colour, shiny blue peacocks and bright green frogs.
zh: 通常答案确实如此。动物世界色彩缤纷,有闪亮的蓝孔雀,也有鲜绿色的青蛙。

text: But in fact, hundreds of animal species are black and white.
zh: 但事实上,有数百种动物是黑白相间的。

text: In this episode, we'll try to find out why, while also learning some useful new words
zh: 在这一期节目中,我们将试着找出原因,同时学习一些实用的新单词和短语。

text: and phrases. And remember, All the vocabulary from this episode is available on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: 记住,本期节目的所有词汇都可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到。

text: OK, time for a question for you, Bekka. According to one theory, some animals are black and white
zh: 好了,该考考你了,Bekka。根据一种理论,有些动物是黑白色的,

text: to warn potential predators that they're dangerous or they taste bad. But what is the scientific term
zh: 是为了警告潜在的捕食者它们很危险或者味道很差。那么这个现象的科学术语是什么呢?

text: for this? Is it a camouflage, b mimicry, or c aposematism?
zh: 是a. 伪装,b. 拟态,还是c. 警戒色?

text: Well, Neil, I'm going to go with C, just because I've never heard of that word before.
zh: 好吧,Neil,我选C,因为我以前从没听说过这个词。

text: OK. I like your style.
zh: 好的。我喜欢你这种选择方式。

text: Some scientists think zebra's distinctive black and white stripes prevent bites from flies and other insects.
zh: 一些科学家认为斑马独特的黑白条纹能防止苍蝇和其他昆虫叮咬。

text: Zoologist Dr Martin Howe tested this theory by covering horses, a close relative of the zebra, in stripes.
zh: 动物学家Martin Howe博士通过给马——斑马的近亲——披上条纹来测试这个理论。

text: Here Martin discusses his experiment with Caroline Steele from BBC World Service programme CrowdScience.
zh: 下面是Martin与BBC世界服务节目《CrowdScience》的Caroline Steele讨论他的实验。

text: Do we know what it is about the stripes that's putting them off? Is it somehow like visually confusing?
zh: 我们知道条纹到底是什么让它们望而却步的吗?是不是在视觉上造成某种混淆?

text: We originally set out to test whether maybe there were these optical illusions at play.
zh: 我们最初是想测试是否存在某种视错觉在起作用。

text: So we did a whole bunch of tests where we tried different patterns of rugs, but it turned out that practically every high contrast pattern,
zh: 所以我们做了一大堆测试,尝试了不同图案的毯子,结果发现几乎每一种高对比度的图案,

text: every black and white pattern we used, Almost every pattern was really effective at stopping flies.
zh: 我们使用的每一种黑白图案,几乎所有图案都能有效阻止苍蝇靠近。

text: Martin conducted a whole bunch of, meaning a lot of, tests using different colours and patterns.
zh: Martin用不同的颜色和图案做了一大堆(意思是很多)测试。

text: Black and white stripes created an optical illusion, a visual trick that makes your eyes see things differently from how they really are.
zh: 黑白条纹造成了一种视错觉,这是一种让你的眼睛看到的东西与实际不同的视觉把戏。

text: As a result, the flies were put off, they lost interest and didn't bite.
zh: 结果,苍蝇被吓退了,它们失去兴趣,不再叮咬。

text: So, zebra's stripes confuse insects, telling them to look elsewhere for food.
zh: 所以,斑马的条纹会迷惑昆虫,让它们去别处寻找食物。

text: But are there other reasons animals are black and white?
zh: 但动物呈现黑白色还有其他原因吗?

text: Nobody who's seen giant pandas in a zoo would find them hard to spot. Against a grassy background, they stand out easily.
zh: 在动物园见过大熊猫的人都不会觉得它们难以发现。在草地背景下,它们非常显眼。

text: But seen from a distance, in the snowy mountain forest where they live, black and white makes the perfect disguise.
zh: 但从远处看,在它们生活的雪山森林中,黑白色恰恰是完美的伪装。

text: Here's Professor Tim Caro, a world authority on animal behaviour,
zh: 接下来是世界知名动物行为学权威Tim Caro教授,

text: explaining more to Caroline Steele for BBC World Service's CrowdScience.
zh: 他向BBC世界服务《CrowdScience》节目的Caroline Steele做了更多解释。

text: The giveaway for me was 15 photos of giant pandas taken either fairly close up or at a distance.
zh: 让我恍然大悟的是15张大熊猫的照片,有的是近距离拍摄,有的是远距离拍摄的。

text: And in two of those photos, I was unable to see anything in the picture other than rocks and snow.
zh: 其中有两张照片,我完全看不出画面里除了岩石和雪之外还有别的东西。

text: And then later on, I realised that in fact there was a giant panda in the centre of that picture, some distance away.
zh: 后来我才意识到,原来那张照片中央,远处确实有一只大熊猫。

text: Immediately that clicked in my mind.
zh: 那一刻,我脑海中豁然开朗。

text: The pandas were so well camouflaged, Tim couldn't spot them in the photos.
zh: 大熊猫伪装得如此巧妙,以至于Tim在照片中根本找不到它们。

text: That's when something clicked in his mind – a phrase used when you suddenly understand something after trying for a long time.
zh: 那就是他“灵光一闪”的时刻——这个短语用来形容经过长时间思考后突然明白了某件事。

text: Tim understood that pandas use colour to hide.
zh: Tim明白了熊猫是利用颜色来隐藏自己的。

text: He calls this a giveaway – something that makes it easy for people to discover something secret or
zh: 他称这个线索为“泄露信息的东西”——指让人们容易发现某个秘密或未知事物的东西。

text: unknown. So, are animals black and white to repel insects, like zebras? Or for camouflage, like pandas.
zh: 那么,动物呈现黑白色到底是为了像斑马那样驱赶昆虫,还是像熊猫那样用于伪装呢?

text: Let's give the final word to Hannah Rowland, a researcher into another black and white animal – penguins.
zh: 让我们把最后的发言权交给研究另一种黑白动物——企鹅——的研究员Hannah Rowland。

text: Hannah Rowland Really, science never has a black and white answer.
zh: Hannah Rowland说,其实科学从来没有非黑即白的答案。

text: Excuse the pun about the… with penguins being black and white.
zh: 请原谅我用企鹅是黑白色这件事来开个双关玩笑。

text: It's often a very complex mixture of reasons.
zh: 这往往是多种复杂因素混合的结果。

text: The answer to why animals are black and white is not black and
zh: 动物为什么是黑白色的答案,并不是非黑即白的。

text: white. A situation described as black and white means one where it's easy to understand what's right and wrong.
zh: 用“非黑即白”来形容一种情况,是指是非对错很容易分辨的情形。

text: In fact, there's no one reason why animals are black and white.
zh: 事实上,动物呈现黑白色并没有单一的原因。

text: Zebras avoid bites, pandas hide from predators, and some animals, like skunks, warn other animals they're dangerous.
zh: 斑马是为了避免叮咬,熊猫是为了躲避捕食者,而像臭鼬这样的动物则是在警告其他动物它们很危险。

text: It all depends on the specific animal. And talking of skunks reminds me of my question, Bekka.
zh: 这完全取决于具体的动物。说到臭鼬,让我想起了我要问你的问题,Bekka。

text: According to one theory, some animals are black and white to warn potential predators, they're dangerous or taste bad.
zh: 根据一种理论,有些动物是黑白色的,是为了警告潜在的捕食者它们很危险或味道很差。

text: But what is the scientific term? And I chose C – opossumatism, because I'd never heard of it before.
zh: 那么这个科学术语是什么?我选了C——“opossumatism”,因为我以前从没听说过。

text: Well, that's a great way of choosing. And it's done well for you because you're correct!
zh: 嗯,这真是个很棒的选择方式。而且这次对你很管用,因为你答对了!

text: Woohoo! OK, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned, starting with optical illusion
zh: 太棒了!好,让我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇,首先是“视错觉”——

text: – a visual trick where your eyes perceive things differently from how they really are.
zh: 一种让你的眼睛看到的事物与实际情况不同的视觉把戏。

text: A whole bunch of something means a lot of something. If something puts you off, it causes you to dislike it or lose interest.
zh: “a whole bunch of”表示“很多”。如果某事让你“put off”,意思是它让你反感或失去兴趣。

text: A giveaway is something that makes it easy for people to discover something secret or unknown.
zh: “giveaway”指的是让人们容易发现某个秘密或未知事物的东西。

text: The phrase something clicked in my mind is used when you suddenly understand something at last.
zh: “something clicked in my mind”这个短语用于形容你终于突然明白了某件事。

text: And finally, if you describe something as black and white, you mean it's easy to understand what is right and wrong.
zh: 最后,如果你把某事描述为“black and white”,意思是是非对错很容易分辨。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up, but head over to our website bbclearningenglish .com
zh: 我们的六分钟又到时间了,不过欢迎前往我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com,

text: for a quiz and worksheet for this episode. See you there soon, but for now it's goodbye.
zh: 获取本期的测验和练习题。我们很快在那里见,现在先说再见。

text: Six Minute English, from bbclearningenglish .com
zh: 《六分钟英语》,来自bbclearningenglish.com。
